prompt: As of June 2026, what are the standards and tools for ML MODEL SUPPLY-CHAIN security and signing? Cover: Sigstore model-signing (model-transparency / OpenSSF Model Signing), in-toto + SLSA for ML, Hugging Face safetensors signing, model cards + EU AI Act technical documentation (Art. 11/53, GPAI Code of Practice), and any registry/attestation standards. Give the actual artifact/envelope formats and adoption status with dates. Cite specs and repos. Then: for a platform that already ships a custom Ed25519 + RFC6962 Merkle + RFC3161 signed ZIP envelope for its model artifacts, exactly how should it align with Sigstore/OpenSSF Model Signing and in-toto/SLSA to be interoperable and 'first-class supply-chain artifact', without abandoning offline verification? Name the concrete format mappings.
content:
**OpenSSF Model Signing (OMS) / Sigstore model-signing (model-transparency) is the leading dedicated standard for ML model integrity and authenticity as of mid-2026.**[[1]](https://openssf.org/blog/2025/06/25/an-introduction-to-the-openssf-model-signing-oms-specification/)[[2]](https://openssf.org/blog/2025/04/04/launch-of-model-signing-v1-0-openssf-ai-ml-working-group-secures-the-machine-learning-supply-chain/)

The project (originally model-transparency) launched v1.0 on April 4, 2025, under the OpenSSF AI/ML Working Group. It provides a library/CLI (`model-signing` package) and the companion **OMS specification** (repo: ossf/model-signing-spec) for signing/verifying any model format or size via a detached signature.[[3]](https://github.com/ossf/model-signing-spec)[[4]](https://github.com/sigstore/model-transparency)

**Artifact/envelope format (OMS/Sigstore bundle)**: A detached Sigstore bundle (protobuf-based; see sigstore/protobuf-specs) containing:
- A **DSSE envelope** (Dead Simple Signing Envelope).
- Inside it, an **in-toto Statement** (`application/vnd.in-toto+json` payloadType) whose subjects are a **manifest** (list of all files in the model with cryptographic hashes, default SHA-256; BLAKE2b also supported). Optional predicates/annotations for metadata (e.g., model version, training details—future expansions planned).
- Signature over the statement.
- Verification material (public key/certificate chain for traditional PKI or bare keys; or Sigstore certificate + Rekor transparency log entry/inclusion proof for keyless signing).[[3]](https://github.com/ossf/model-signing-spec)[[5]](https://next.redhat.com/2025/04/10/model-authenticity-and-transparency-with-sigstore/)[[5]](https://next.redhat.com/2025/04/10/model-authenticity-and-transparency-with-sigstore/)

It is PKI-agnostic: supports Sigstore keyless (Fulcio + Rekor), self-signed certs, bare Ed25519/RSA/etc. keys, enterprise PKI, or PKCS#11. The same hashing/signature format is used regardless. Verification can be offline when the bundle includes all material.[[6]](https://openssf.org/projects/model-signing/)

**Adoption status (as of mid-2026)**: NVIDIA signs *all* published models in its NGC Catalog with OMS (announced ~July 2025). Google has integrated/prototyped it with Kaggle Model Hub. It is the de facto standard promoted by OpenSSF/Sigstore for ML supply chains, with broad industry backing (Google, NVIDIA, HiddenLayer, etc.).[[7]](https://www.hiddenlayer.com/insight/openssf-model-signing-for-safer-ai-supply-chains)[[8]](https://openssf.org/blog/2025/07/23/case-study-google-secures-machine-learning-models-with-sigstore/)

**in-toto + SLSA for ML**: OMS is explicitly built on in-toto attestations (the manifest is an in-toto Statement inside DSSE). SLSA provenance attestations use the same in-toto format. SLSA itself is mature for software builds but ML-specific extensions (e.g., full pipeline provenance covering data/training steps) remain emerging/gapped as of 2023–2026 discussions; OMS provides a practical bridge for model artifacts.[[9]](https://slsa.dev/blog/2023/05/in-toto-and-slsa)[[10]](https://www.reversinglabs.com/blog/ai-and-transparency-how-ml-model-creators-can-protect-against-supply-chain-attacks)

**Hugging Face safetensors signing**: Safetensors (zero-copy, pickle-free tensor format) emphasizes runtime safety against arbitrary code execution and received a positive external audit in 2023. It is widely adopted on the Hub (millions of models). There is no native/standard cryptographic signing of model origin or supply-chain provenance in the core library or Hub (focus is on deserialization safety). Community/third-party experiments exist (e.g., wrappers adding signatures), but these are not standardized. OMS/model-signing tools can sign safetensors files or collections containing them.[[11]](https://huggingface.co/blog/safetensors-security-audit)

**Model cards + EU AI Act technical documentation (Art. 11/53, GPAI Code of Practice)**: 
- **Art. 11** requires technical documentation for high-risk AI systems (Annex IV details).
- **Art. 53** (GPAI models) requires up-to-date technical documentation on training/testing/evaluation processes and results (minimum per Annex XI), plus transparency info for downstream integrators (Annex XII). This aligns closely with model cards (intended use, architecture, data, eval results, limitations, etc.).
- The voluntary **GPAI Code of Practice** (published July 10, 2025; endorsed as adequate for compliance presumption) covers transparency, copyright, and (for systemic-risk models) safety/security chapters. Signatories (many major providers) can use adherence to demonstrate Art. 53/55 compliance. It complements but does not replace cryptographic signing; signed artifacts + docs strengthen evidence.[[12]](https://artificialintelligenceact.eu/article/53/)[[13]](https://digital-strategy.ec.europa.eu/en/policies/contents-code-gpai)

**Registries/attestation standards**: Major model hubs (NGC, Kaggle) now attach OMS signatures. Broader ecosystem uses OCI registries for models as artifacts with attached Sigstore bundles/in-toto attestations/SBOMs (AI-SBOM/MBOM extensions emerging via SPDX/CycloneDX). SLSA + Sigstore is the recommended attestation stack; no single universal ML registry standard beyond these.

**Aligning a custom Ed25519 + RFC6962 Merkle + RFC3161 signed ZIP envelope** (for interoperability as a “first-class supply-chain artifact” while preserving offline verification):

Your custom format (per-file or tree-hashed ZIP with Ed25519 signatures, Merkle tree per RFC 6962 for efficient verification of subsets, and RFC 3161 timestamps) provides strong offline integrity/authenticity. To align without abandoning it:

1. **Produce a parallel/derived OMS/Sigstore bundle** (or convertible artifact) at publish time:
   - Generate the OMS manifest (in-toto Statement listing all files + their hashes—reuse your existing hash computations).
   - Wrap in DSSE.
   - Sign the DSSE payload using your existing Ed25519 key (or a derived one) and embed verification material (public key or cert chain). For Sigstore compatibility, optionally route through a Sigstore instance if keyless is desired later.
   - Wrap the DSSE in a Sigstore bundle (includes any timestamp/proof material; your RFC 3161 timestamp can map into the bundle or an attestation predicate).
   - Output both your original signed ZIP *and* the `.sigstore`/OMS `.bundle` (or a sidecar file). Hubs/tools expecting OMS will consume the bundle; your users keep the ZIP.[[3]](https://github.com/ossf/model-signing-spec)[[5]](https://next.redhat.com/2025/04/10/model-authenticity-and-transparency-with-sigstore/)

2. **Concrete format mappings**:
   - **Manifest/subjects**: Direct 1:1—your ZIP file list + hashes → OMS in-toto Statement subjects (ResourceDescriptors with digests).
   - **Signature layer**: Map your Ed25519 signature over the (Merkle) root or manifest to the DSSE signature field. The Merkle tree can be represented as an annotation or separate predicate if needed for subset verification.
   - **Timestamping**: Embed RFC 3161 TSA token/response in the Sigstore bundle’s verification material or as an in-toto predicate (or link via log entry). This preserves non-repudiation.
   - **Envelope**: Your signed ZIP remains the primary artifact. The OMS bundle is detached (standard for Sigstore/OMS) and references the same files by hash.
   - **Offline verification**: Fully supported—Sigstore bundles are designed for this (include all certs/proofs). Verifiers can check your custom ZIP first, then fall back to/ cross-validate against the OMS bundle using standard Sigstore/in-toto tooling.
   - **Interoperability bonuses**: The resulting bundle is consumable by `cosign`, `model-signing verify`, Rekor, etc., and satisfies SLSA-style provenance when combined with build attestations. Registries/hubs adopting OMS will treat it as first-class.

3. **Implementation path**: Extend your signing pipeline to also emit the OMS manifest + DSSE/bundle (libraries like sigstore-python or the model-signing package make this straightforward; reuse your Ed25519 key). Publish both artifacts. Document the mapping for verifiers. This keeps your custom format as the “source of truth” while adding standards-compliant sidecars.

This approach ensures your artifacts are verifiable with both legacy/custom tools and the emerging OMS/Sigstore/in-toto ecosystem, meeting supply-chain expectations from model hubs, SLSA, and regulatory contexts (e.g., signed docs + artifacts strengthen EU AI Act technical documentation evidence) without requiring users to abandon offline checks.[[7]](https://www.hiddenlayer.com/insight/openssf-model-signing-for-safer-ai-supply-chains)

Key repos/specs to cite: ossf/model-signing-spec, sigstore/model-transparency (or model-signing), sigstore/protobuf-specs (bundle), in-toto/attestation, slsa.dev.
